content.claim: Earlier age of selection and number of school-types are significantly associated with higher inequality of opportunity across OECD countries; applied to Hungary, selective tracks cream-skim the best students at ages 10 and 12 rather than the official age 14, and recent administrative data confirm school segregation by social status remains among the highest in Europe.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Political feasibility lens
limitations: Administrative data studies measure association, not isolated causal effect of track structure alone
Segregation is multiply determined (residential segregation, free choice, tracked system); attribution of causal weight to any single factor is not provided
Comparison to 'other European countries' does not specify which or provide exact rankings
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
